schools.summary: Gill: the writer describes himself first by his name, Peter, the same with Cephas, which signifies a rock or stone, a name given him by Christ at his first conversion and which respected his after firmness, solidity, resolution, and constancy; Peter writing as an apostle of Jesus Christ to the strangers scattered through Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia — the salutation to the dispersed pilgrims of the faith (Peter, naming himself 'an apostle of Jesus Christ,' one sent by Christ's own authority, addressing his letter to 'the strangers scattered,' the believers, chiefly converted Jews, dispersed through the provinces of Asia Minor, who were 'strangers' both as foreigners scattered from their homeland and, more deeply, as pilgrims and sojourners in this world, their true home being heaven, so the letter is addressed to a suffering, dispersed people, to comfort and establish them in their pilgrim faith).

themes: Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ
to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia
the salutation to dispersed pilgrims
strangers from their homeland and in the world

schools.summary: Gill: the persons addressed are 'elect' — not to any office, as bishops or presbyters, for the apostle does not write to all under that character; nor as a nation, for they were scattered persons; nor as a church; but personally and individually chosen, according to the foreknowledge of God the Father; Peter describing them as elect according to the foreknowledge of the Father, through the sanctification of the Spirit, unto obedience and the sprinkling of Christ's blood — the threefold divine work that brings the elect to salvation (Peter setting the whole Trinity behind the believers' salvation: they are 'elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father,' chosen in God's eternal, loving foreknowledge, 'through sanctification of the Spirit,' set apart and made holy by the Holy Spirit's work, 'unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ,' the end being a life of obedience and the cleansing application of Christ's atoning blood; the rich order, that the Father chooses, the Spirit sanctifies, the Son's blood is sprinkled, each Person concurring in the believer's salvation, and Peter praying grace and peace be multiplied).

sides.position: According to the eternal, loving foreknowledge of God the Father — personally and individually chosen, the whole Trinity concurring: the Father choosing, the Spirit sanctifying, the Son's blood sprinkled

themes: elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father
through sanctification of the Spirit
unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ
the trinitarian work behind election

schools.summary: Gill: the epistle begins with thanksgiving to God, an ascription of blessing, praise, and glory to him; this does not mean an invoking or conferring a blessing on him (neither of which can be, for there is not a greater than he to be invoked); Peter blessing the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who according to his abundant mercy has begotten us again to a living hope by Christ's resurrection — the praise to God for the new birth unto a living hope grounded in the resurrection (Peter opening with adoration: 'blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ,' the ground of the praise being the great mercy of regeneration: God 'according to his abundant mercy,' the rich overflowing mercy that is the only spring of it, 'hath begotten us again,' the new birth making us his children, 'unto a lively hope,' a living vigorous hope of glory, 'by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead'; the believer's hope being 'lively,' alive and sure, precisely because it rests on the risen Christ, whose resurrection is the pledge and ground of the believer's own).

themes: blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
according to his abundant mercy hath begotten us again unto a lively hope
by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead
the hope alive and sure, resting on the risen Christ

schools.summary: Gill: this is a further explanation of the 'lively hope,' or hope laid up in heaven, which regenerate ones are begotten to: it is an 'inheritance,' a large estate and rich possession, to which they are born heirs apparent, not got by industry or obtained by works but by grace; Peter describing the inheritance as incorruptible, undefiled, unfading, and reserved in heaven — the imperishable, kept heavenly inheritance of the believer (Peter setting out the glorious object of the living hope: an 'inheritance,' a possession the believer holds not by his own works but as a born heir of God, and marking its surpassing qualities by three negatives that set it above all earthly estates: 'incorruptible,' not subject to decay or destruction as earthly things are, 'and undefiled,' pure, unstained, untouched by any defilement, 'and that fadeth not away,' never withering or losing its beauty unlike the fairest earthly flower, and its security: 'reserved in heaven for you,' kept safe and laid up in heaven beyond all reach of loss, so the believer's portion is an imperishable, pure, unfading, and securely-kept heavenly inheritance).

themes: to an inheritance incorruptible, and undefiled, and that fadeth not away
reserved in heaven for you
the imperishable heavenly inheritance
incorruptible, pure, unfading, securely kept

schools.summary: Gill: this is a description of the persons for whom the inheritance is reserved in heaven; they are not only chosen to salvation and begotten again to an inheritance, but they are preserved unto it; their happiness is very great, their inheritance is safe in heaven for them, and they are kept safe on earth; Peter declaring that they are kept by the power of God through faith unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time — the double security, the inheritance kept for them and they kept for it (Peter completing the believer's security: not only is the inheritance 'reserved in heaven' but the heirs themselves 'are kept by the power of God,' guarded and preserved by God's almighty power that they may not fall away or be lost, 'through faith,' the means by which God keeps them, the grace through which his power works, 'unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time'; the perfect safety of the believer, the inheritance kept for them and they kept for the inheritance, both secured by the power of God until the full salvation is revealed at the last day, so the saints' final perseverance rests on God's omnipotence not their own strength).

debates.question: On what does the believer's final perseverance rest?

sides.position: On God's almighty power — the heirs are 'kept by the power of God' through faith, that they may not fall away, until salvation is revealed; their security rests on God's omnipotence, not their own strength

themes: who are kept by the power of God through faith
unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time
the heirs kept by God's almighty power
perseverance resting on God's omnipotence

schools.summary: Gill: the saints greatly rejoice in their salvation, hope, and inheritance, though now for a season, if need be, they are in heaviness through manifold temptations (the Vulgate reads 'in which ye shall rejoice,' referring it to the last time, when the saints will greatly rejoice in full possession of eternal salvation); Peter observing that they greatly rejoice in this, though now for a season, if need be, they are in heaviness through manifold trials — the joy of salvation held even amid the grief of many trials (Peter joining joy and sorrow in the believer's present state: 'wherein ye greatly rejoice,' in the salvation, hope, and inheritance just described the saints having a deep and abiding joy, 'though now for a season,' the trials being but for a short time, 'if need be,' only so far as God sees necessary, never beyond need, 'ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations,' made sorrowful by various trials and afflictions; the balance of grace, that the believer rejoices in his sure salvation even while grieved by present trials, the joy being deep and lasting, the heaviness brief and measured, sent only as far as needful, so grief and joy may dwell together in the suffering saint).

themes: wherein ye greatly rejoice
though now for a season, if need be
ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations
joy deep and lasting, heaviness brief and measured

schools.summary: Gill: this is the principal end God has in afflictive providences, to try the faith of his people, as the faith of Abraham, Job, and Habakkuk was tried; Barnes: the putting of one's religion to the test, showing what is genuine; Peter declaring that the trial of their faith, more precious than perishing gold tried by fire, might be found unto praise and honour and glory at Christ's appearing — the refining purpose of trial, that proven faith may issue in glory (Peter revealing the gracious design behind the heaviness: 'that the trial of your faith,' the testing and proving of their faith by affliction, and marking its worth: 'being much more precious than of gold that perisheth, though it be tried with fire,' faith proved being more precious than gold, for gold even refined by fire perishes but proven faith is imperishable, and its glorious issue: 'might be found unto praise and honour and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ'; trial, like the goldsmith's fire, purifying faith and proving it genuine, and such tested faith being openly rewarded with praise, honour, and glory when Christ appears, so present trials are the refining of a faith destined for glory).

themes: that the trial of your faith, being much more precious than of gold that perisheth
though it be tried with fire
might be found unto praise and honour and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ
trial refining a faith destined for glory

schools.summary: Gill: Jesus Christ, whom they had never seen with their bodily eyes, being Jews who dwelt not in Judea when Christ was upon earth but were scattered about in the Gentile world, yet, Christ being made known to them through the preaching of the gospel, they loved him; Peter declaring that, having not seen Christ, they love him, and believing in him though they see him not, they rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory — the love and inexpressible joy of faith fixed on the unseen Saviour (Peter marking the wonder of the believers' faith and affection: 'whom having not seen, ye love,' they never beholding Christ with bodily eyes yet truly loving him, 'in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing,' resting on him by faith not sight, 'ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory'; the heights of this joy, a joy that words cannot utter, 'unspeakable,' and that is already touched with the glory to come, 'full of glory,' springing not from sight but from faith and love toward the unseen Christ, so the believer's love and joy rest not on seeing but on believing).

themes: whom having not seen, ye love
in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing
ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory
love and joy resting on believing, not sight

schools.summary: Gill: 'salvation' intends spiritual and eternal salvation, that which God appointed his people to from all eternity, which is obtained by Christ, applied by the Spirit, and will be fully enjoyed hereafter, which is a just and sufficient ground of joy and rejoicing; Peter declaring that they are receiving the end of their faith, even the salvation of their souls — the salvation of the soul as the goal and present possession of faith (Peter naming what makes that joy so well-grounded: 'receiving the end of your faith,' faith's great aim, design, and issue, 'even the salvation of your souls'; the present tense, that believers are even now 'receiving' this salvation, possessing it in part by faith and certain to enjoy it in full, for the very purpose toward which faith looks and presses is the eternal salvation of the soul, which God appointed, Christ obtained, and the Spirit applies, so faith does not press toward an uncertain prize but is even now laying hold of its sure and glorious end, the saving of the soul).

themes: receiving the end of your faith
even the salvation of your souls
the salvation of the soul as faith's great end
faith laying hold of its sure and glorious end

schools.summary: Gill: the prophets greatly desired the coming of the Saviour and to see him; they longed after the salvation to be accomplished by him, expressed their wishes for him, inquired into its nature, and gave an account thereof according to the measure given them; Peter declaring that the prophets enquired and searched diligently into this salvation, prophesying of the grace that should come — the earnest search of the prophets into the salvation now revealed (Peter, to magnify the greatness of the gospel salvation, pointing to how the prophets longed and laboured over it: 'of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently,' they making earnest careful inquiry, longing to understand the salvation they foretold, 'who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you'; the honour this puts on the believers' gospel, that the very prophets who foretold it did not fully possess it but searched into it as into a deep and precious mystery, prophesying of the 'grace' that was to come to the gospel-age church, so the salvation now enjoyed was the object of the prophets' longing study).

themes: of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently
who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you
the prophets' diligent search into the coming salvation
the gospel longed for and studied by prophets

schools.summary: Gill: the prophets made a very diligent inquiry into the exact time when Christ should come to work out the salvation of his people, to whom it was made known that his coming should be before the sceptre or tribe of Judah and all civil government in it ceased; Peter declaring that they searched what time the Spirit of Christ in them signified, testifying beforehand the sufferings of Christ and the glory to follow — the Spirit of Christ in the prophets foretelling his sufferings and glory (Peter revealing both the object and the author of the prophets' search: they searched 'what, or what manner of time,' when and in what circumstances the salvation should come, and the One who moved and informed them was 'the Spirit of Christ which was in them,' the Holy Spirit, here called the Spirit of Christ, indwelling and inspiring the prophets, who 'did signify, when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow'; the two great themes the Spirit foretold, first 'the sufferings of Christ,' his passion and death, and then 'the glory that should follow,' his resurrection, exaltation, and kingdom, so the Old Testament prophets, by Christ's own Spirit, foretold both his cross and his crown).

debates.question: Whose Spirit moved and inspired the Old Testament prophets?

sides.position: The Spirit of Christ — the Holy Spirit, here called the Spirit of Christ, indwelling and inspiring the prophets, foretelling beforehand both Christ's sufferings and the glory to follow

themes: searching what, or what manner of time the Spirit of Christ which was in them did signify
the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow
the Spirit of Christ in the prophets foretelling cross and crown
Christ's own Spirit inspiring the prophets

schools.summary: Gill: it was revealed to the prophets the salvation they searched into and the time of its being wrought out, what sort of times they would be when Christ should come, and what cruel sufferings the Messiah should undergo; Peter declaring that it was revealed to the prophets that they ministered not to themselves but to us, things now preached by the gospel with the Holy Ghost, which the angels desire to look into — the prophets serving the gospel church, and the angels' wondering longing (Peter showing the prophets were shown they served a future age: 'not unto themselves, but unto us they did minister the things,' their prophecies being for the benefit of the gospel-age church, 'which are now reported unto you by them that have preached the gospel unto you with the Holy Ghost sent down from heaven,' the same things now openly preached in the Spirit's power, and adding a striking note of the glory of this gospel: 'which things the angels desire to look into'; the wonder, that even the holy angels stoop and gaze with eager longing into the mysteries of redemption, as into something surpassing and ever new, so the gospel the believers possess is a thing prophets served and angels marvel at).

themes: not unto themselves, but unto us they did minister the things
preached the gospel... with the Holy Ghost sent down from heaven
which things the angels desire to look into
prophets serving the gospel church, angels marvelling

schools.summary: Gill: 'gird up the loins of your mind' with the girdle of truth; since angels desire to look into the mysteries of grace, the saints should apply their minds and diligently attend to them, in opposition to all loose and vagrant thoughts of the mind, giving themselves up wholly to them; Peter charging that they gird up the loins of their mind, be sober, and hope to the end for the grace to be brought at Christ's revelation — the call to a girded, sober mind and a hope fixed firmly on the coming grace (Peter, drawing the practical conclusion from the glory of their salvation, calling for three things: 'gird up the loins of your mind,' like a man girding his loose robes to run or work, so brace and collect the mind for action free of loose distracting thoughts, 'be sober,' self-controlled, watchful, temperate, and 'hope to the end,' set a firm persevering hope, 'for the grace that is to be brought unto you at the revelation of Jesus Christ'; the believer fixing his hope wholly and steadfastly on the grace and glory to be brought at Christ's appearing, with a mind girded and sober for the journey there).

themes: wherefore gird up the loins of your mind, be sober
hope to the end
for the grace that is to be brought unto you at the revelation of Jesus Christ
a girded, sober mind and a steadfast hope

schools.summary: Gill: 'as obedient children,' or 'children of obedience'; seeing they were children of God by adopting grace and in regeneration brought to the obedience of faith, to whom the inheritance belonged, they ought to live accordingly; Peter charging that, as obedient children, they not fashion themselves according to their former lusts in their ignorance — the call of God's children to be unlike their old sinful selves (Peter, pressing holiness, addressing the believers by their new character: 'as obedient children,' children of God marked by obedience, born to a new life of submission to him, and bidding them not to be 'fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts,' not shaping their lives by the sinful desires that ruled them before, 'in your ignorance'; the contrast between the old life and the new, that those lusts belonged to the time of their 'ignorance,' before they knew God, and are not to be the pattern of the obedient child's life now, so conversion makes a clean break with the former sinful mould).

themes: as obedient children
not fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts
in your ignorance
conversion making a clean break with the old mould

schools.summary: Gill: 'he which hath called you is holy' is a description of God the Father, who had called them not merely in an external way by the outward ministry of the word but internally, powerfully, and efficaciously by his Spirit and grace, and who had called them to holiness of life and conversation; Peter charging that, as he who called them is holy, so they be holy in all manner of conduct — the holiness of God set as the pattern of the believer's whole life (Peter giving the positive side of the charge and its great motive: 'but as he which hath called you is holy,' the God who effectually called them being himself holy, perfectly pure, 'so be ye holy in all manner of conversation,' be holy too, and that not in part but in the whole manner of life, in every action and relation; the loftiness of the standard, that the believer's holiness is to be patterned on God's own holiness, the holy God calling his people to be like himself, and it reaching 'all manner of conversation,' every part of life, not some narrow corner only, so the called are to bear the family likeness of their holy God in all they do).

themes: but as he which hath called you is holy
so be ye holy in all manner of conversation
holiness patterned on the holy God who called
the called bearing their God's family likeness

schools.summary: Barnes: this command, 'Be ye holy; for I am holy' (Leviticus 11:44), was addressed at first to the Israelites, but is with equal propriety addressed to Christians as the professed people of God; the foundation of the command is that they professed to be his; Peter grounding the call to holiness in the written word, 'Be ye holy; for I am holy' — the divine command and reason for holiness drawn from Scripture (Peter clinching his exhortation by an appeal to the law: 'because it is written, Be ye holy; for I am holy,' citing God's own word in Leviticus; the force of this, that the call to holiness is no mere apostolic counsel but God's standing command, and the reason given is the highest possible, God's own holiness, 'for I am holy,' so the believer's holiness is required by the express word of God and grounded in the very nature of the God whose people they are, they must being holy because he who owns them is holy).

themes: because it is written, Be ye holy
for I am holy
the scriptural command and reason for holiness
grounded in God's own holiness

schools.summary: Gill: 'if ye call on the Father,' or 'seeing' ye do; this is a fresh argument engaging to holiness of life; invocation of God includes the whole worship of him, the performance of every outward duty and the exercise of every inward grace, particularly designing prayer; Peter charging that, since they call on the Father who judges impartially according to each man's work, they pass their sojourning here in fear — the reverent fear that befits those who own an impartial Father and Judge (Peter drawing another motive to holiness from the character of the God they call Father: 'if ye call on the Father,' since you invoke and worship God as your Father, 'who without respect of persons judgeth according to every man's work,' who is no respecter of persons but judges each impartially by his works, therefore 'pass the time of your sojourning here in fear'; the kind of fear meant, not slavish dread but a reverent, holy, filial fear, a careful walking that dreads to offend so holy and impartial a Father, and 'the time of your sojourning here' marking the believer's brief pilgrimage on earth, to be spent in this reverent fear).

themes: if ye call on the Father, who without respect of persons judgeth
pass the time of your sojourning here in fear
reverent fear before the impartial Father and Judge
a filial fear dreading to offend so holy a Father

schools.summary: Gill: they knew this from the Scriptures of truth, by the testimony of the Spirit, by his work upon the soul, and by the application of the benefits of redemption (justification, pardon, adoption, sanctification); Barnes: this is an argument for a holy life, derived from the manner of their redemption; Peter reasoning that they know they were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold, from their vain manner of life inherited from their fathers — the costliness of redemption as a motive for holiness (Peter pressing holiness by the priceless means of their redemption: 'forasmuch as ye know,' it is a known and certain truth, 'that ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold,' no perishable price however precious bought them, 'from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers,' delivered from the empty worthless manner of life they had inherited, the traditional but vain way of their ancestors; the force, that to be bought out of so worthless a former life at no common price binds the redeemed to holiness, silver and gold, the most precious of perishing things, not able to redeem them, which sets up the contrast of the next verse).

themes: forasmuch as ye know that ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold
from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers
the costliness of redemption a motive for holiness
not bought by perishable silver or gold

schools.summary: Gill: Christ was prophesied of as a Redeemer under the Old Testament, and the Jews frequently ascribe redemption to the word of the Lord God; the apostle here attributes redemption to the blood of Christ, whose blood is the same with ours, only not tainted with sin, the blood of an innocent and spotless Lamb; Peter declaring that they were redeemed with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot — the priceless blood of the spotless Lamb as the ransom-price (Peter naming the true and infinitely precious price of redemption, in glorious contrast to silver and gold: 'but with the precious blood of Christ,' the blood of the Son of God of inestimable worth and dignity, 'as of a lamb without blemish and without spot'; the rich typology, that Christ is the true Paschal and sacrificial Lamb, answering to the spotless lambs of the law that had to be 'without blemish,' for he was perfectly sinless, holy, and pure, and it was this precious spotless blood, not corruptible metal, that purchased the believers' redemption, so the costliness of the ransom both magnifies redemption and binds the redeemed to holy living).

themes: but with the precious blood of Christ
as of a lamb without blemish and without spot
the precious blood of the spotless Lamb as the ransom
Christ the true Paschal Lamb, sinless and pure

schools.summary: Gill: 'foreordained,' or 'foreknown,' that is by God, which intends not barely his prescience of Christ, of what he should be, do, and suffer, but such a previous knowledge joined with love and affection to him, not merely as his own Son and the express image of his person but as Mediator; Peter declaring that Christ was foreordained before the foundation of the world, but manifest in these last times for them — the eternal foreordination of Christ the Redeemer, revealed in the last times (Peter setting the redeeming Lamb in the vast span of God's eternal purpose: Christ 'verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world,' designated and set apart as the Redeemer in God's eternal counsel, before time began, no afterthought but the eternal plan, 'but was manifest in these last times for you'; the two ages joined, that Christ was eternally ordained to be the sacrifice yet only 'manifest,' made openly known, having come in the flesh and accomplished redemption, 'in these last times,' the gospel age, and that 'for you,' for the very believers Peter addresses, so the Lamb of God was both eternally appointed and now historically revealed, all for his people's sake).

themes: who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world
but was manifest in these last times for you
the eternal foreordination of Christ the Redeemer
no afterthought but the eternal plan, revealed for his people

schools.summary: Gill: Christ, as God, is the object of faith; as Mediator, he is the way to the Father, by which men come to him, believe in him, and lay hold upon him as their covenant God and Father, and is also the author of that faith by which they believe; Peter declaring that by Christ they believe in God, who raised him from the dead and gave him glory, that their faith and hope might be in God — faith and hope reaching God through the risen and glorified Christ (Peter showing how Christ brings his people to God: it is 'by him,' through Christ, the only way to the Father, that they 'do believe in God,' and the God they believe in is the One 'that raised him up from the dead, and gave him glory,' who raised and exalted Christ, the resurrection being God's seal on his finished work, and the gracious purpose: 'that your faith and hope might be in God'; the resurrection and exaltation of Christ being the very ground on which the believer's faith and hope rest securely in God, for the God who raised and glorified the Redeemer is the sure object of their trust).

themes: who by him do believe in God, that raised him up from the dead, and gave him glory
that your faith and hope might be in God
faith and hope reaching God through the risen Christ
the resurrection the ground of trust in God

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle passes to another exhortation, namely to brotherly love, the ground of which he makes to be the purification of their souls, which supposes that they had been impure, and indeed their whole persons, souls and bodies, were so by nature; Peter charging that, having purified their souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit unto unfeigned love, they love one another with a pure heart fervently — the fervent, sincere brotherly love that flows from a purified soul (Peter grounding the duty of love in the work already wrought in them: 'seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit,' their souls cleansed by the Spirit's power through their obedient reception of the gospel, 'unto unfeigned love of the brethren,' the very aim and fruit of that purifying being a genuine, unhypocritical love of fellow believers, therefore 'see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently'; the two marks of this love, that it is to be from 'a pure heart,' sincere and cleansed of selfishness and guile, and 'fervently,' warm, earnest, intense, not cold or slack, so the purified soul is to overflow in warm, sincere love to the brethren).

themes: seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit
unto unfeigned love of the brethren
see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently
fervent, sincere love flowing from a purified soul

schools.summary: Gill: they were born again of God, according to his abundant mercy, by the resurrection of Christ, to a lively hope of a glorious inheritance, and therefore, being brethren in a spiritual relation, they ought to love as brethren, being children of the same Father and belonging to the same family; Peter declaring that they are born again, not of corruptible but of incorruptible seed, by the word of God which lives and abides for ever — the new birth by the abiding word a further ground of brotherly love (Peter giving a further and deeper ground for brotherly love, their common new birth: 'being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible,' regenerated not by the perishable seed of natural generation but by an imperishable spiritual seed, 'by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever'; the means and seed of this new birth being 'the word of God,' the living and abiding gospel, which the Spirit uses to beget souls anew, and because that word is itself living and eternal, the life it produces is likewise imperishable, so those born again of the same incorruptible seed and abiding word are truly brethren, bound to love one another).

themes: being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible
by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever
the new birth by the incorruptible seed of the word
the life imperishable, like the abiding word

schools.summary: Gill: all men, as born of corruptible seed, are frail, mortal, and perishing; they spring up like grass and look beautiful for a while but are very weak and tender, and in a little time are cut down by death and wither away, and while they live are in a good measure nothing but grass in another form; Peter citing that all flesh is as grass and all the glory of man as the flower of grass, which withers and falls away — the fading of all earthly things set against the abiding word (Peter, having spoken of the imperishable word, setting against it the perishableness of all human things, citing Isaiah: 'all flesh is as grass,' man in his whole natural being frail, short-lived, like grass that springs up and is gone, 'and all the glory of man as the flower of grass,' even man's finest glory, beauty, and achievement like the brief flower of the field, 'the grass withereth, and the flower thereof falleth away'; the lesson, that all that is of nature, the man and his glory alike, quickly fades and dies, so there is nothing abiding to rest upon in the flesh, the contrast preparing the great affirmation of the next verse that the word alone endures).

themes: for all flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the flower of grass
the grass withereth, and the flower thereof falleth away
the fading of all flesh and human glory
nothing abiding in the flesh

schools.summary: Gill: though men die, and ministers of the word too, and everything in the world is uncertain, unstable, fleeting, and passing away, and whatever change has been in the ordinances of divine service, yet the word of the Lord, the gospel of Christ, is settled for ever and will continue; Peter declaring that the word of the Lord endures for ever, and that this is the word preached to them by the gospel — the imperishable, abiding word now preached as the seed of the new birth (Peter completing the contrast: against all the fading of flesh and glory stands the changeless word, 'but the word of the Lord endureth for ever,' the gospel, God's word, abiding unchanging while all else passes, and applying it: 'and this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you'; the comfort and force, that the very word now preached to them in the gospel is that eternal, abiding word of God by which they were born again, so that, while all the world withers, the believer is begotten and built upon a word that never passes away, the sure and lasting foundation of his hope).

themes: but the word of the Lord endureth for ever
this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you
the imperishable, abiding word of the Lord
the very gospel by which they were born again
